---
title: "Primers for AD698 - Applied Generative AI for Analytics"
subtitle: "Primer 4 - Embeddings as Meaning"
author: "Nakul R. Padalkar"
institute: "Boston University"
number-sections: false
date: "2026-01-01"
date-modified: today
date-format: long
engine: jupyter
bibliography: ../../references.bib
categories: ['Embeddings', 'Semantic Search', 'Foundations', 'Embeddings as Meaning']
description: "Introduces embeddings as meaning as a foundation for applied generative AI concepts, technical vocabulary, and later course workflows."
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    fontsize: 10pt
    css: primer-style.css
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Embeddings as Meaning

An embedding is a learned numerical representation of an object. In language
models, tokens, words, sentences, documents, images or user queries can be
represented as vectors. These vectors are useful because objects with similar
usage, meaning or function tend to occupy nearby regions of the vector space.

Embeddings do not contain meaning in a philosophical sense. They encode
statistical relationships learned from data. However, those relationships are
often useful enough to support semantic search, clustering, recommendation,
classification, retrieval-augmented generation and change detection across
large text collections.
The modern embedding story connects older vector-space models in information
retrieval with neural distributional representations such as word2vec and GloVe
[@Salton.Wong.Yang.1975; @TurneyPantel2010VSM; @Mikolov2013Word2Vec;
@Pennington.Socher.Manning.2014].

::: {.key-box}
**Key idea.** Embeddings convert qualitative artifacts into quantitative
geometry. Once text becomes vectors, similarity, distance, neighborhoods and
clusters become computable.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-embedding-panels
#| fig-cap: "Three views of embeddings. (a) Related texts occupy nearby regions in a vector space. (b) Cosine similarity compares vector direction rather than raw length. (c) Retrieval ranks documents by similarity between a query vector and document vectors."
#| fig-width: 8.2
#| fig-height: 2.65
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(11)
plt.rcParams.update({
    "font.size": 8,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "figure.dpi": 140
})

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(8.2, 2.65))

# Panel a: semantic neighborhoods
ax = axes[0]
centers = {
    "finance": np.array([0.28, 0.68]),
    "support": np.array([0.66, 0.62]),
    "legal": np.array([0.48, 0.28])
}
colors = {"finance": "#2563eb", "support": "#047857", "legal": "#f97316"}
for label, center in centers.items():
    pts = np.random.normal(center, [0.055, 0.045], size=(12, 2))
    ax.scatter(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], s=13, color=colors[label], alpha=0.85)
    ax.text(center[0] - 0.06, center[1] + 0.09, label, color=colors[label], fontsize=8)
ax.set_xlim(0.12, 0.82)
ax.set_ylim(0.12, 0.86)
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel("embedding dimension 1")
ax.set_ylabel("embedding dimension 2")
ax.set_title("Neighborhoods encode relatedness", pad=4)
ax.text(0.14, 0.81, "a", fontweight="bold")

# Panel b: cosine similarity
ax = axes[1]
ax.set_aspect("equal")
origin = np.array([0, 0])
v1 = np.array([0.85, 0.45])
v2 = np.array([0.62, 0.73])
v3 = np.array([-0.15, 0.92])
for v, color, label in [(v1, "#2563eb", "query"), (v2, "#047857", "doc A"), (v3, "#9ca3af", "doc B")]:
    ax.arrow(0, 0, v[0], v[1], head_width=0.035, length_includes_head=True, color=color, lw=1.4)
    ax.text(v[0] * 1.08, v[1] * 1.08, label, color=color, fontsize=8)
theta = np.linspace(np.arctan2(v1[1], v1[0]), np.arctan2(v2[1], v2[0]), 25)
ax.plot(0.25 * np.cos(theta), 0.25 * np.sin(theta), color="#111827", lw=1.0)
ax.text(0.23, 0.20, "small angle", fontsize=7)
ax.set_xlim(-0.25, 1.05)
ax.set_ylim(-0.10, 1.05)
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_title("Similarity is geometric", pad=4)
ax.text(-0.20, 0.98, "b", fontweight="bold")

# Panel c: retrieval ranking
ax = axes[2]
docs = ["security\nreview", "budget\napproval", "renewal\nemail", "office\nlease", "product\nroadmap"]
scores = [0.86, 0.63, 0.58, 0.31, 0.22]
order = np.arange(len(docs))
ax.barh(order, scores, color=["#047857", "#2563eb", "#2563eb", "#9ca3af", "#9ca3af"])
ax.set_yticks(order)
ax.set_yticklabels(docs)
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_xlim(0, 1)
ax.set_xlabel("cosine similarity to query")
ax.set_title("Nearest vectors are retrieved", pad=4)
ax.text(0.02, -0.55, "c", fontweight="bold")

fig.tight_layout(w_pad=1.0)
plt.show()
```

**Figure 1a** shows a simplified two-dimensional projection of an embedding
space. Real embeddings may have hundreds or thousands of dimensions, but the
intuition is similar: texts about related topics tend to form neighborhoods.
This makes clustering and semantic search possible even when documents do not
share exact keywords.

**Figure 1b** shows cosine similarity. Many embedding systems compare vectors
by the angle between them:

$$
\begin{align}
\cos(\theta)
  &= \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\| \|b\|}.
\end{align}
$$

Two vectors can have different lengths but similar directions. Cosine
similarity therefore emphasizes orientation in the embedding space rather than
raw magnitude.

**Figure 1c** shows the retrieval setting. A query is embedded into the same
space as documents. The system retrieves document vectors nearest to the query
vector. These retrieved documents can then be passed to a language model as
evidence.

# What Embeddings Learn

Embeddings are learned from prediction tasks. A model is trained so that useful
relationships in data become reflected in vector geometry. For word-level
models, words appearing in similar contexts receive similar vectors. For modern
sentence or document embeddings, entire passages are trained so that
semantically related texts are nearby.

The phrase "nearby" is doing important work. It does not mean identical. It
means similar according to the model's training objective and data. Two
documents can be nearby because they discuss the same topic, express the same
intent, answer the same question, or share a genre.

# Distributional Meaning

The basic linguistic intuition is distributional: expressions that occur in
similar contexts tend to have related meanings. Neural embeddings operationalize
this idea by learning vectors that are useful for prediction. In word2vec, for
example, the training task links a word to its surrounding context; in GloVe,
the objective is tied to global co-occurrence statistics
[@Mikolov2013Word2Vec; @Pennington.Socher.Manning.2014]. Modern sentence and
document embeddings extend this idea from words to larger units of text.

This does not make embeddings transparent. Individual dimensions usually do not
correspond cleanly to human-interpretable features. Meaning is distributed
across the vector, and similarity emerges from the geometry of many dimensions
taken together.

# From Sparse to Dense Representations

Traditional information retrieval often represents documents as sparse vectors.
For example, a bag-of-words or TF-IDF vector has one dimension per vocabulary
term, and most values are zero. Sparse methods are strong when exact terms
matter.

Embeddings are dense vectors. Most dimensions contain nonzero values, and each
dimension is usually not directly interpretable. Dense vectors are strong when
semantic similarity matters.

| Representation | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword search | Exact matching, transparency | misses paraphrases |
| TF-IDF/BM25 | strong lexical retrieval baseline | limited semantic generalization |
| Dense embeddings | captures paraphrase and related meaning | can retrieve plausible but wrong passages |
| Hybrid retrieval | combines lexical and semantic signals | more system complexity |

For business uses, hybrid retrieval is often attractive. Exact terms such as
policy IDs, product names, invoice numbers and legal clauses matter, but so does
semantic similarity.

The practical lesson is not to replace lexical retrieval everywhere. Sparse
retrieval is often better for identifiers, quotes and exact terminology. Dense
retrieval is often better for paraphrase and intent. Hybrid systems recognize
that natural language contains both exact strings and flexible meaning.

# Embeddings in RAG

Retrieval-augmented generation usually follows this sequence:

1. Split source documents into chunks.
2. Embed each chunk.
3. Store vectors in a vector index.
4. Embed the user query.
5. Retrieve nearby chunks.
6. Send retrieved evidence to the language model.
7. Generate an answer grounded in that evidence.

Embeddings therefore act as the bridge between a natural-language question and
the documents likely to answer it. A poor embedding model, poor chunking
strategy or poor similarity threshold can cause the language model to receive
the wrong evidence.

::: {.pitfall-box}
**Pitfall.** Retrieval quality is upstream of generation quality. If the
retrieved passages are irrelevant, incomplete or misleading, a fluent answer may
still be wrong.
:::

# Semantic Similarity Is Task Dependent

There is no single universal definition of similarity. Consider the query:

> delayed customer renewal

Possible matches include:

- a support ticket about unresolved security review;
- a sales note about procurement delay;
- a contract clause about renewal windows;
- a finance record showing late payment;
- a product note about missing integration features.

All may be related, but not equally useful for every task. If the task is
revenue forecasting, the finance record may matter most. If the task is
customer-success intervention, the support and sales notes may matter most. If
the task is legal risk review, the contract clause may matter most.

Embedding systems must therefore be evaluated against the actual business
question, not only against generic semantic similarity.

# Clustering, Drift and Change Detection

Embeddings are useful beyond retrieval. Once documents are vectors, an analyst
can cluster them, track movement over time and detect changes in themes.

Examples:

- cluster support tickets to discover emerging issues;
- track whether customer complaints shift after a product release;
- compare job descriptions before and after an organizational change;
- detect policy language that is semantically similar but not lexically
  identical;
- identify duplicate or near-duplicate documents.

These workflows are powerful because they treat text as analyzable data. They
also require validation. A cluster may reflect writing style, template reuse or
source system conventions rather than a meaningful business theme.

# Limitations

Embeddings compress information. Compression makes search efficient, but it can
hide details. Important qualifiers, negations, dates, numbers and jurisdictional
conditions may not dominate the vector. For example, "approved for internal
use" and "not approved for external use" may be semantically close while having
opposite compliance implications.

Embedding spaces can also reproduce biases from training data. They may place
terms, occupations or demographic references into neighborhoods that reflect
historical stereotypes. For high-stakes uses, embedding behavior should be
audited with representative queries and documents.

# Granularity

The object being embedded matters. A token embedding, sentence embedding,
paragraph embedding and document embedding answer different questions.

| Unit embedded | Good for | Common problem |
|---|---|---|
| token | internal model computation | context changes representation |
| sentence | semantic similarity | may omit document-level context |
| paragraph/chunk | RAG retrieval | boundary choices affect evidence |
| document | clustering and overview | loses fine-grained answer spans |

Many retrieval failures are really granularity failures. The correct document
may be retrieved, but the answer-bearing sentence may be buried. Or a highly
specific sentence may be retrieved without the surrounding context needed to
interpret it.

# Business Use Without Losing the NLP Core

| Design choice | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| embedding model | determines the geometry of similarity |
| chunk size | controls context and retrieval granularity |
| similarity metric | defines what "near" means |
| top-k retrieval | controls how much evidence enters the prompt |
| reranking | can improve ordering after initial retrieval |
| metadata filters | enforce business constraints such as date or department |
| evaluation set | reveals whether retrieval supports the actual task |

The strongest retrieval systems are rarely just "embed and search." They use
metadata, lexical matching, semantic matching, reranking, thresholds and human
inspection during development.

# Demonstration: A Query Is Not a Topic Label

A query such as `delayed customer renewal` is not a stable topic label. It is a
short linguistic object whose nearest neighbors depend on the embedding model,
document chunks and similarity metric.

| Candidate passage | Why it may rank highly | Why it may fail |
|---|---|---|
| security review note | strong paraphrase of delay cause | may be one account only |
| procurement email | semantically related process delay | may not explain renewal |
| renewal clause | exact term overlap | may be legal boilerplate |
| product roadmap | customer context overlap | may be irrelevant to delay |

The retrieval question is therefore not "what is nearest?" but "what nearest
passages are sufficient for the downstream answer?"

::: {.takeaway-box}
**Takeaway.** Embeddings make language computable as geometry. They are central
to semantic search, clustering and RAG, but they must be evaluated as part of a
task-specific retrieval system.
:::

### References

Classic foundations include vector-space retrieval [@Salton.Wong.Yang.1975],
survey treatments of distributional semantics [@TurneyPantel2010VSM],
word2vec [@Mikolov2013Word2Vec], GloVe
[@Pennington.Socher.Manning.2014], and the embeddings chapter in Jurafsky and
Martin [@JurafskyMartin2026SLP].
